sections.label: Section 265
sections.original: οἷ γʼ αὐτῷ κακὰ τεύχει ἀνὴρ ἄλλῳ κακὰ τεύχων ,
sections.translation: He does mischief to himself who does mischief to another , and evil planned harms the plotter most . The eye of Zeus , seeing all and understanding all , beholds these things too , if so he will , and fails not to mark what sort of justice is this that the city keeps within it .

sections.label: Section 270
sections.original: νῦν δὴ ἐγὼ μήτʼ αὐτὸς ἐν ἀνθρώποισι δίκαιος
sections.translation: Now , therefore , may neither I myself be righteous among men , nor my son — for then it is a bad thing to be righteous — if indeed the unrighteous shall have the greater right . But I think that all-wise Zeus will not yet bring that to pass . 274 But you , Perses , lay up these things within your heart and

sections.label: Section 275
sections.original: καὶ νυ δίκης ἐπάκουε , βίης δʼ ἐπιλήθεο πάμπαν .
sections.translation: listen now to right , ceasing altogether to think of violence . For the son of Cronos has ordained this law for men , that fishes and beasts and winged fowls should devour one another , for right is not in them ; but to mankind he gave right which proves

sections.label: Section 280
sections.original: γίγνεται · εἰ γάρ τίς κʼ ἐθέλῃ τὰ δίκαιʼ ἀγορεῦσαι
sections.translation: far the best . For whoever knows the right and is ready to speak it , far-seeing Zeus gives him prosperity ; but whoever deliberately lies in his witness and foreswears himself , and so hurts Justice and sins beyond repair , that man ' s generation is left obscure thereafter .

sections.label: Section 285
sections.original: ἀνδρὸς δʼ εὐόρκου γενεὴ μετόπισθεν ἀμείνων .
sections.translation: But the generation of the man who swears truly is better thenceforward . To you , foolish Perses , I will speak good sense . Badness can be got easily and in shoals ; the road to her is smooth , and she lives very near us . But between us and Goodness the gods have placed the sweat of our brows ;

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: ἀθάνατοι · μακρὸς δὲ καὶ ὄρθιος οἶμος ἐς αὐτὴν
sections.translation: long and steep is the path that leads to her , and it is rough at the first ; but when a man has reached the top , then is she easy to reach , though before that she was hard .

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: ἐσθλὸς δʼ αὖ κἀκεῖνος , ὃς εὖ εἰπόντι πίθηται ·
sections.translation: That man is altogether best who considers all things himself and marks what will be better afterwards and at the end ; and he , again , is good who listens to a good adviser ; but whoever neither thinks for himself nor keeps in mind what another tells him , he is an unprofitable man . But do you at any rate , always remembering my charge , work , high-born Perses , that Hunger

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: ἐχθαίρῃ , φιλέῃ δέ σʼ ἐυστέφανος Δημήτηρ
sections.translation: may hate you , and venerable Demeter richly crowned may love you and fill your barn with food ; for Hunger is altogether a meet comrade for the sluggard . Both gods and men are angry with a man who lives idle ,

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: οἵ τε μελισσάων κάματον τρύχουσιν ἀεργοὶ
sections.translation: for in nature he is like the stingless drones who waste the labor of the bees , eating without working ; but let it be your care to order your work properly , that in the right season your barns may be full of victual . Through work men grow rich in flocks and substance , and

sections.label: Section 309
sections.original: καὶ ἐργαζόμενοι πολὺ φίλτεροι ἀθανάτοισιν .
sections.translation: working they are much better loved by the immortals . [*]

sections.label: Section 311
sections.original: ἔργον δʼ οὐδὲν ὄνειδος , ἀεργίη δέ τʼ ὄνειδος .
sections.translation: Work is no disgrace : it is idleness which is a disgrace . But if you work , the idle will soon envy you as you grow rich , for fame and renown attend on wealth . And whatever be your lot , work is best for you ,

sections.label: Section 315
sections.original: εἴ κεν ἀπʼ ἀλλοτρίων κτεάνων ἀεσίφρονα θυμὸν
sections.translation: if you turn your misguided mind away from other men ' s property to your work and attend to your livelihood as I bid you . An evil shame is the needy man ' s companion , shame which both greatly harms and prospers men : shame is with poverty , but confidence with wealth . 320

sections.label: Section 320
sections.original: χρήματα δʼ οὐχ ἁρπακτά , θεόσδοτα πολλὸν ἀμείνω .
sections.translation: Wealth should not be seized : god-given wealth is much better ; for if a man takes great wealth violently and perforce , or if he steals it through his tongue , as often happens when gain deceives men ' s sense and dishonor tramples down honor ,

sections.label: Section 325
sections.original: ῥεῖα δέ μιν μαυροῦσι θεοί , μινύθουσι δὲ οἶκον
sections.translation: the gods soon blot him out and make that man ' s house low , and wealth attends him only for a little time . Alike with him who does wrong to a suppliant or a guest , or who goes up to his brother ' s bed and commits unnatural sin in lying with his wife ,

sections.label: Section 330
sections.original: ὅς τέ τευ ἀφραδίῃς ἀλιταίνεται ὀρφανὰ τέκνα ,
sections.translation: or who infatuately offends against fatherless children , or who abuses his old father at the cheerless threshold of old age and attacks him with harsh words , truly Zeus himself is angry , and at the last lays on him a heavy requital for his evil doing .

sections.label: Section 335
sections.original: ἀλλὰ σὺ τῶν μὲν πάμπαν ἔεργʼ ἀεσίφρονα θυμόν .
sections.translation: But do you turn your foolish heart altogether away from these things , and , as far as you are able , sacrifice to the deathless gods purely and cleanly , and burn rich meats also , and at other times propitiate them with libations and incense , both when you go to bed and when the holy light has come back ,

sections.label: Section 340
sections.original: ὥς κέ τοι ἵλαον κραδίην καὶ θυμὸν ἔχωσιν ,
sections.translation: that they may be gracious to you in heart and spirit , and so you may buy another ' s holding and not another yours . Call your friend to a feast ; but leave your enemy alone ; and especially call him who lives near you : for if any mischief happens in the place ,

sections.label: Section 345
sections.original: γείτονες ἄζωστοι ἔκιον , ζώσαντο δὲ πηοί .
sections.translation: neighbors come ungirt , but kinsmen stay to gird themselves . [*] A bad neighbor is as great a plague as a good one is a great blessing ; he who enjoys a good neighbor has a precious possession . Not even an ox would die but for a bad neighbor . Take fair measure from your neighbor and pay him back

sections.label: Section 350
sections.original: αὐτῷ τῷ μέτρῳ , καὶ λώιον , αἴ κε δύνηαι ,
sections.translation: fairly with the same measure , or better , if you can ; so that if you are in need afterwards , you may find him sure . Do not get base gain : base gain is as bad as ruin . Be friends with the friendly , and visit him who visits you . Give to one who gives , but do not give to one who does not give .

sections.label: Section 355
sections.original: δώτῃ μέν τις ἔδωκεν , ἀδώτῃ δʼ οὔτις ἔδωκεν .
sections.translation: A man gives to the free-handed , but no one gives to the closefisted . Give is a good girl , but Take is bad and she brings death . For the man who gives willingly , even though he gives a great thing , rejoices in his gift and is glad in heart ; but whoever gives way to shamelessness and takes something himself ,

sections.label: Section 360
sections.original: καί τε σμικρὸν ἐόν , τό γʼ ἐπάχνωσεν φίλον ἦτορ .
sections.translation: even though it is a small thing ,

sections.label: Section 363
sections.original: ὃς δʼ ἐπʼ ἐόντι φέρει , ὃ δʼ ἀλέξεται αἴθοπα λιμόν ·
sections.translation: it freezes his heart . He who adds to what he has , will keep off bright-eyed hunger ;

sections.label: Section 361
sections.original: εἰ γάρ κεν καὶ σμικρὸν ἐπὶ σμικρῷ καταθεῖο
sections.translation: for if you add only a little to a little and do this often ,

sections.label: Section 362
sections.original: καὶ θαμὰ τοῦτʼ ἔρδοις , τάχα κεν μέγα καὶ τὸ γένοιτο .
sections.translation: soon that little will become great . What a man has by him at home does not trouble him :

sections.label: Section 365
sections.original: οἴκοι βέλτερον εἶναι , ἐπεὶ βλαβερὸν τὸ θύρηφιν .
sections.translation: it is better to have your stuff at home , for whatever is abroad may mean loss . It is a good thing to draw on what you have ; but it grieves your heart to need something and not to have it , and I bid you mark this . Take your fill when the cask is first opened and when it is nearly spent , but midways be sparing : it is poor saving when you come to the lees . 370

sections.label: Section 370
sections.original: μισθὸς δʼ ἀνδρὶ φίλῳ εἰρημένος ἄρκιος ἔστω .
sections.translation: Let the wage promised to a friend be fixed ; even with your brother smile — and get a witness ; for trust and mistrust alike ruin men . Do not let a flaunting woman coax and cozen and deceive you : she is after your barn . The man who trusts womankind trusts deceivers .

sections.label: Section 375
sections.original: ὃς δὲ γυναικὶ πέποιθε , πέποιθʼ ὅ γε φηλήτῃσιν .
sections.translation: There should be an only son to feed his father ' s house , for so wealth will increase in the home ; but if you leave a second son you should die old . Yet Zeus can easily give great wealth to a greater number .

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: πλείων μὲν πλεόνων μελέτη , μείζων δʼ ἐπιθήκη .
sections.translation: More hands mean more work and more increase . If your heart within you desires wealth , do these things and work with work upon work . When the Pleiades , daughters of Atlas , are rising , [*] begin your harvest , and your ploughing when they are going to set . [*]

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: αἳ δή τοι νύκτας τε καὶ ἤματα τεσσαράκοντα
sections.translation: Forty nights and days they are hidden and appear again as the year moves round , when first you sharpen your sickle . This is the law of the plains , and of those who live near the sea ,

sections.label: Section 390
sections.original: πόντου κυμαίνοντος ἀπόπροθι , πίονα χῶρον
sections.translation: and who inhabit rich country , the glens and hollows far from the tossing sea ,— strip to sow and strip to plough and strip to reap , if you wish to get in all Demeter ' s fruits in due season , and that each kind may grow in its season . Else , afterwards , you may chance to be in want ,

sections.label: Section 395
sections.original: πτώσσῃς ἀλλοτρίους οἴκους καὶ μηδὲν ἀνύσσῃς .
sections.translation: and go begging to other men ' s houses , but without avail ; as you have already come to me . But I will give you no more nor give you further measure . Foolish Perses ! Work the work which the gods ordained for men , lest in bitter anguish of spirit you with your wife and children

sections.label: Section 400
sections.original: ζητεύῃς βίοτον κατὰ γείτονας , οἳ δʼ ἀμελῶσιν .
sections.translation: seek your livelihood amongst your neighbors , and they do not heed you . Two or three times , may be , you will succeed , but if you trouble them further , it will not avail you , and all your talk will be in vain , and your word-play unprofitable . Nay , I bid you find a way to pay your debts and avoid hunger . 405

sections.label: Section 405
sections.original: οἶκον μὲν πρώτιστα γυναῖκά τε βοῦν τʼ ἀροτῆρα ,
sections.translation: First of all , get a house , and a woman and an ox for the plough — a slave woman and not a wife , to follow the oxen as well — and make everything ready at home , so that you may not have to ask of another , and he refuse you , and so , because you are in lack , the season pass by and your work come to nothing .

sections.label: Section 410
sections.original: μηδʼ ἀναβάλλεσθαι ἔς τʼ αὔριον ἔς τε ἔνηφιν ·
sections.translation: Do not put your work off till to-morrow and the day after ; for a sluggish worker does not fill his barn , nor one who puts off his work : industry makes work go well , but a man who puts off work is always at hand-grips with ruin . When the piercing power and sultry heat of the sun abate ,

sections.label: Section 415
sections.original: καύματος ἰδαλίμου , μετοπωρινὸν ὀμβρήσαντος
sections.translation: and almighty Zeus sends the autumn rains , [*] and men ' s flesh comes to feel far easier ,— for then the star Sirius passes over the heads of men , who are born to misery , only a little while by day and takes greater share of night —

sections.label: Section 420
sections.original: τῆμος ἀδηκτοτάτη πέλεται τμηθεῖσα σιδήρῳ
sections.translation: then , when it showers its leaves to the ground and stops sprouting , the wood you cut with your axe is least liable to worm . Then remember to hew your timber : it is the season for that work . Cut a mortar [*] three feet wide and a pestle three cubits long , and an axle of seven feet , for it will do very well so ;

sections.label: Section 425
sections.original: εἰ δέ κεν ὀκταπόδην , ἀπὸ καὶ σφῦράν κε τάμοιο .
sections.translation: but if you make it eight feet long , you can cut a beetle [*] from it as well . Cut a felloe three spans across for a wagon of ten palms ' width . Hew also many bent timbers , and bring home a plough-tree when you have found it , and look out on the mountain or in the field for one of holm-oak ;

sections.label: Section 430
sections.original: εὖτʼ ἂν Ἀθηναίης δμῷος ἐν ἐλύματι πήξας
sections.translation: for this is the strongest for oxen to plough with when one of Athena ' s handmen has fixed in the share-beam and fastened it to the pole with dowels . Get two ploughs ready and work on them at home , one all of a piece , and the other jointed . It is far better to do this , for if you should break one of them , you can put the oxen to the other .

sections.label: Section 435
sections.original: δάφνης δʼ ἢ πτελέης ἀκιώτατοι ἱστοβοῆες ,
sections.translation: Poles of laurel or elm are most free from worms , and a share-beam of oak and a plough-tree of holm-oak . Get two oxen , bulls of nine years ; for their strength is unspent and they are in the prime of their age : they are best for work . They will not fight in the furrow and break the plough

sections.label: Section 440
sections.original: ἄξειαν , τὸ δὲ ἔργον ἐτώσιον αὖθι λίποιεν .
sections.translation: and then leave the work undone . Let a brisk fellow of forty years follow them , with a loaf of four quarters [*] and eight slices [*] for his dinner , one who will attend to his work and drive a straight furrow and is past the age for gaping after his fellows ,

sections.label: Section 445
sections.original: θυμὸν ἔχων · τοῦ δʼ οὔτι νεώτερος ἄλλος ἀμείνων
sections.translation: but will keep his mind on his work . No younger man will be better than he at scattering the seed and avoiding double-sowing ; for a man less staid gets disturbed , hankering after his fellows . 448 Mark , when you hear the voice of the crane [*] who cries year by year from the clouds above ,

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: ἥτʼ ἀρότοιό τε σῆμα φέρει καὶ χείματος ὥρην
sections.translation: for she gives the signal for ploughing and shows the season of rainy winter ; but she vexes the heart of the man who has no oxen . Then is the time to feed up your horned oxen in the byre ; for it is easy to say : “ Give me a yoke of oxen and a wagon ,” and it is easy to refuse : “ I have work for my oxen .”

sections.label: Section 455
sections.original: φησὶ δʼ ἀνὴρ φρένας ἀφνειὸς πήξασθαι ἄμαξαν ,
sections.translation: The man who is rich in fancy thinks his wagon as good as built already — the fool ! he does not know that there are a hundred timbers to a wagon . Take care to lay these up beforehand at home . So soon as the time for ploughing is proclaimed to men , then make haste , you and your slaves alike ,

sections.label: Section 460
sections.original: αὔην καὶ διερὴν ἀρόων ἀρότοιο καθʼ ὥρην ,
sections.translation: in wet and in dry , to plough in the season for ploughing , and bestir yourself early in the morning so that your fields may be full . Plough in the spring ; but fallow broken up in the summer will not belie your hopes . Sow fallow land when the soil is still getting light : fallow land is a defender from harm and a soother of children .

sections.label: Section 465
sections.original: εὔχεσθαι δὲ Διὶ χθονίῳ Δημήτερί θʼ ἁγνῇ ,
sections.translation: Pray to Zeus of the Earth and to pure Demeter to make Demeter ' s holy grain sound and heavy , when first you begin ploughing , when you hold in your hand the end of the plough-tail and bring down your stick on the backs of the oxen as they draw on the pole-bar by the yoke-straps .

sections.label: Section 470
sections.original: δμῷος ἔχων μακέλην πόνον ὀρνίθεσσι τιθείη
sections.translation: Let a slave follow a little behind with a mattock and make trouble for the birds by hiding the seed ; for good management is the best for mortal men as bad management is the worst . In this way your corn-ears will bow to the ground with fullness if the Olympian himself gives a good result at the last ,

sections.label: Section 475
sections.original: ἐκ δʼ ἀγγέων ἐλάσειας ἀράχνια · καί σε ἔολπα
sections.translation: and you will sweep the cobwebs from your bins and you will be glad , I think , as you take of your garnered substance . And so you will have plenty till you come to grey [*] springtime , and will not look wistfully to others , but another shall be in need of your help . 479 But if you plough the good ground at the solstice , [*]

sections.label: Section 480
sections.original: ἥμενος ἀμήσεις ὀλίγον περὶ χειρὸς ἐέργων ,
sections.translation: you will reap sitting , grasping a thin crop in your hand , binding the sheaves awry , dust-covered , not glad at all ; so you will bring all home in a basket and not many will admire you . Yet the will of Zeus who holds the aegis is different at different times ; and it is hard for mortal men to tell it ;

sections.label: Section 485
sections.original: εἰ δέ κεν ὄψʼ ἀρόσῃς , τόδε κέν τοι φάρμακον εἴη ·
sections.translation: for if you should plough late , you may find this remedy — when the cuckoo first calls [*] in the leaves of the oak and makes men glad all over the boundless earth , if Zeus should send rain on the third day and not cease until it rises neither above an ox ' s hoof nor falls short of it ,

sections.label: Section 490
sections.original: οὕτω κʼ ὀψαρότης πρῳηρότῃ ἰσοφαρίζοι .
sections.translation: then the late-plougher will vie with the early . Keep all this well in mind , and fail not to mark grey spring as it comes and the season of rain . Pass by the smithy and its crowded lounge in winter time when the cold keeps men from field work ,—

sections.label: Section 495
sections.original: ἰσχάνει , ἔνθα κʼ ἄοκνος ἀνὴρ μέγα οἶκον ὀφέλλοι ,
sections.translation: for then an industrious man can greatly prosper his house — lest bitter winter catch you helpless and poor , and you chafe a swollen foot with a shrunk hand .

sections.label: Section 500
sections.original: ἐλπὶς δʼ οὐκ ἀγαθὴ κεχρημένον ἄνδρα κομίζει ,
sections.translation: The idle man who waits on empty hope , lacking a livelihood , lays to heart mischief-making ; it is not a wholesome hope that accompanies a needy man who lolls at ease while he has no sure livelihood . 504 While it is yet midsummer command your slaves : “ It will not always be summer , build barns .” Avoid the month Lenaeon , [*] wretched days , all of them fit to skin an ox ,

sections.label: Section 505
sections.original: τοῦτον ἀλεύασθαι , καὶ πηγάδας , αἵτʼ ἐπὶ γαῖαν
sections.translation: and the frosts which are cruel when Boreas blows over the earth . He blows across horse-breeding Thrace upon the wide sea and stirs it up , while earth and the forest howl . On many a high-leafed oak and thick pine he falls

sections.label: Section 510
sections.original: οὔρεος ἐν βήσσῃς πιλνᾷ χθονὶ πουλυβοτείρῃ
sections.translation: and brings them to the bounteous earth in mountain glens : then all the immense wood roars and the beasts shudder and put their tails between their legs , even those whose hide is covered with fur ; for with his bitter blast he blows even through them , although they are shaggy-breasted .

sections.label: Section 515
sections.original: καί τε διὰ ῥινοῦ βοὸς ἔρχεται , οὐδέ μιν ἴσχει ·
sections.translation: He goes even through an ox ' s hide ; it does not stop him . Also he blows through the goat ' s fine hair . But through the fleeces of sheep , because their wool is abundant , the keen wind Boreas pierces not at all ; but it makes the old man curved as a wheel . And it does not blow through the tender maiden

sections.label: Section 520
sections.original: ἥτε δόμων ἔντοσθε φίλῃ παρὰ μητέρι μίμνει
sections.translation: who stays indoors with her dear mother , unlearned as yet in the works of golden Aphrodite , and who washes her soft body and anoints herself with oil and lies down in an inner room within the house , on a winter ' s day when the Boneless One [*] gnaws his foot

sections.label: Section 525
sections.original: ἔν τʼ ἀπύρῳ οἴκῳ καὶ ἤθεσι λευγαλέοισιν .
sections.translation: in his fireless house and wretched home ; for the sun shows him no pastures to make for , but goes to and fro over the land and city of dusky men , [*] and shines more sluggishly upon the whole race of the Hellenes . Then the horned and unhorned denizens of the wood ,

sections.label: Section 530
sections.original: λυγρὸν μυλιόωντες ἀνὰ δρία βησσήεντα
sections.translation: with teeth chattering pitifully , flee through the copses and glades , and all , as they seek shelter , have this one care , to gain thick coverts or some hollow rock . Then , like the Three-legged One [*] whose back is broken and whose head looks down upon the ground ,

sections.label: Section 535
sections.original: τῷ ἴκελοι φοιτῶσιν , ἀλευόμενοι νίφα λευκήν .
sections.translation: like him , I say , they wander to escape the white snow . 536 Then put on , as I bid you , a soft coat and a tunic to the feet to shield your body ,— and you should weave thick woof on thin warp . In this clothe yourself so that your hair may keep still

sections.label: Section 540
sections.original: μηδʼ ὀρθαὶ φρίσσωσιν ἀειρόμεναι κατὰ σῶμα .
sections.translation: and not bristle and stand upon end all over your body . Lace on your feet close-fitting boots of the hide of a slaughtered ox , thickly lined with felt inside . And when the season of frost comes on , stitch together skins of firstling kids with ox-sinew , to put over your back
